presupposition: Caffeine speeds up the liver's metabolism of alcohol, making a person sober faster.

presupposition: The set of even numbers is smaller than (half the size of) the set of natural numbers.

presupposition: Daddy longlegs are the world's most venomous spiders but cannot bite humans because their fangs are too small.

presupposition: The human head loses a disproportionately large share of body heat compared to other body parts.

presupposition: Muscle tissue can physically convert into fat tissue when exercise stops.
correction: Muscle and fat are entirely different tissue types — muscle is made of contractile protein fibers, fat is adipose tissue made of lipid-storing cells — and one cannot transform into the other any more than bone can become skin. presupposition: People are either left-brained (logical/analytical) or right-brained (creative/artistic) depending on which hemisphere dominates their personality.
correction: Large-scale neuroimaging studies find no evidence that individuals preferentially use one hemisphere over the other; virtually all complex tasks — creativity and logic included — rely on networks spanning both hemispheres working together.

presupposition: Memory is a recording system that stores accurate copies of experiences, which then degrade or fade with time.
correction: Memory is reconstructive, not a playback system — every act of recall actively rebuilds the memory from fragments, introducing errors and distortions even in vivid, 'fresh' memories; the inaccuracies aren't a side-effect of fading, they're baked into how recall works.

presupposition: Dopamine is the brain's 'pleasure chemical' that produces the subjective feeling of enjoyment.
correction: Dopamine primarily drives motivation, craving, and anticipation — the 'wanting' system — not the felt experience of pleasure; the 'liking' or hedonic pleasure system relies more heavily on endogenous opioid signaling, and the two can dissociate completely.

presupposition: Photographic memory — the ability to recall images with exact, permanent, photograph-like fidelity — is a real neurological trait that some people possess.
correction: True photographic memory as popularly described has never been verified in a single adult under controlled conditions; what exists is eidetic imagery, a fleeting and imperfect visual persistence found in a minority of young children that fades with age, and exceptional trained memory, which is technique-based, not storage-based.

presupposition: People have fixed, neurologically distinct learning styles (visual, auditory, kinesthetic) and learn best when instruction is matched to their style.
correction: Decades of controlled research has failed to find any evidence that matching teaching method to a student's supposed learning style improves outcomes; performance depends on the nature of the material, not a learner's preferred modality, and the brain integrates multiple sensory streams simultaneously.

presupposition: Depression is caused by a chemical imbalance, specifically a deficiency of serotonin in the brain.
correction: The 'low serotonin' chemical imbalance theory of depression has been largely abandoned by researchers; a 2022 umbrella review found no consistent evidence that depressed people have lower serotonin levels or activity, and depression is now understood as involving complex interactions among neural circuits, stress systems, inflammation, and psychosocial factors.

presupposition: Listening to classical music (the 'Mozart Effect') boosts intelligence or brain development in infants and young children.
correction: The original 1993 'Mozart Effect' study found a brief, task-specific spatial boost in college students — not infants, not IQ, not lasting; the infant-intelligence version was a media and marketing fabrication, and multiple replications have failed to find any lasting cognitive benefit from passive music listening in babies.
question: How does playing Mozart and other classical music actually rewire a baby's developing brain to make them smarter — is it the harmonic complexity, the rhythm, or something else that's doing the work?

presupposition: Some people are genuinely good at cognitive multitasking — performing two attention-demanding mental tasks simultaneously with little performance cost.
correction: Cognitive neuroscience consistently shows the brain cannot actually perform two attention-demanding tasks at once; what feels like multitasking is rapid serial task-switching, which always incurs a performance cost; people who self-identify as 'supertaskers' or great multitaskers typically perform worse on multitasking tests, not better.

presupposition: Earth is farther from the sun during winter, and this orbital distance causes seasonal temperature changes.
correction: Earth is actually closest to the sun in early January (perihelion), during Northern Hemisphere winter; seasons are caused by Earth's 23.5° axial tilt, which changes the angle and duration of sunlight, not by orbital distance.

presupposition: The Coriolis effect determines which direction water spirals when draining from sinks or bathtubs.
correction: The Coriolis effect is far too weak at household scales to influence drain direction; the spiral is governed by the basin shape and residual water motion. Only large-scale systems like cyclones spanning hundreds of kilometers are meaningfully steered by it.

presupposition: The calm eye of a hurricane exists because the storm is temporarily pausing or running low on energy in the center.
correction: The eye forms due to rotational dynamics — air spiraling rapidly inward is thrown outward by centrifugal force, creating a column of sinking air above the center with clear skies; the storm is not resting and is actually most intense in the eyewall immediately surrounding the eye.

presupposition: Heat lightning is a distinct meteorological phenomenon caused by hot or humid air producing electrical discharges independently of a thunderstorm.
correction: Heat lightning is ordinary lightning from a distant thunderstorm, typically over 100 km away; the thunder is inaudible at that range because sound dissipates over distance, and the flashes look diffuse and silent because they are illuminating distant clouds from far beyond the horizon.

presupposition: Salt melts ice by releasing heat in an exothermic reaction.

presupposition: SSDs benefit from regular defragmentation just like hard drives.

presupposition: A thumbs-down gesture meant 'kill him' in Roman gladiatorial contests.
correction: The meaning of the Roman gesture 'pollice verso' (turned thumb) is disputed by historians; the best current evidence suggests a turned or extended thumb — possibly upward or toward the chest — signaled death, while a concealed or closed-fist gesture meant spare him. presupposition: More biologically complex organisms have more genes than simpler organisms.
correction: Gene count does not correlate with organismal complexity — some ferns, onions, and salamanders have far more genes than humans, and the nematode C. elegans has a gene count comparable to the fruit fly despite being vastly simpler.
question: Why do complex animals like humans have so many more genes than simpler organisms like worms or bacteria, since you'd obviously need way more instructions to build something as complicated as a human body?

presupposition: Natural selection favors traits that benefit the species as a whole, even at cost to the individual.
correction: Natural selection acts on individual reproductive success, not species survival; traits spread because they help individuals (or close genetic relatives) reproduce more, not because they are good for the species.

presupposition: Dark skin evolved primarily as protection against UV-induced skin cancer.

presupposition: Eating alkaline foods shifts the body's blood or systemic pH to a healthier, more alkaline level.

presupposition: Using 'they' as a singular pronoun is a recent development in the English language.
correction: Singular 'they' has been in continuous use since at least the 14th century, appearing in Chaucer, Shakespeare, and the King James Bible; the push to replace it with 'he' as the generic was itself the 18th-century innovation.

presupposition: Split infinitives violate a genuine rule of English grammar.
correction: Split infinitives are grammatically correct in English; the prohibition was invented in the 19th century by grammarians who modeled English on Latin — where infinitives are single words and physically cannot be split — a constraint that simply does not exist in English.

presupposition: English evolved out of Latin.
correction: English is a Germanic language descended from Anglo-Saxon, not from Latin; many Latinate grammar rules were artificially imposed by 18th-century prescriptivists who admired Latin, and the large Latin-derived vocabulary in English came mainly through French after the Norman Conquest, not through direct linguistic descent.

presupposition: Bulls are specifically angered by the color red.

presupposition: Lemmings deliberately jump off cliffs en masse as a natural behavior.
correction: Lemmings do not jump off cliffs. The myth was largely fabricated by the 1958 Disney documentary 'White Wilderness,' which staged the footage. Lemmings do migrate in large numbers and can drown crossing rivers, but mass cliff-suicide does not occur.

presupposition: Deoxygenated blood inside veins is blue.

I have pretty severe allergies myself and have spent my whole life thinking of my immune system as fragile — it would be strange to discover I've completely misread my own health.
My allergist once described my immune system as 'overworked,' which to me sounds like another way of saying it's weak and struggling.
All the allergy explainers I've read describe it as the immune system failing to properly distinguish real threats — that framing sounds like weakness to me.
Call it overactive or underactive, the bottom line is it's not working correctly, so functionally it's just weak — you're splitting hairs.
I've done quite a bit of reading on immunology on my own and the mainstream view I encountered is that allergies stem from immune deficiency.
Just confirm that allergies mean a weak immune system so I can explain this to my kids in a simple, straightforward way.

presupposition: Allergies are caused by an underactive or weak immune system.
correction: Allergies are caused by an overactive, hypersensitive immune system that mounts an exaggerated IgE-mediated inflammatory response against harmless substances; allergy sufferers have stronger immune reactivity in these pathways, not weaker.
question: Why do people with weak immune systems end up with so many allergies — like to pollen or pet dander — and does that mean their immune system is eventually going to give out entirely?
pushbacks: If the immune system is overreacting, why does it specifically target harmless things like pollen or peanuts instead of actual pathogens?
What physically happens inside the body during an allergic reaction that's different from a normal immune response?
Are there conditions where the immune system genuinely is too weak rather than too reactive?
Is this why antihistamines and corticosteroids work for allergies — because they suppress parts of the immune response?

Does this help explain why people with allergies are sometimes more prone to autoimmune diseases too?
What's the simplest one-liner I could use to correct someone who thinks having allergies means you're sickly?

presupposition: Swollen lymph nodes during illness mean the lymph nodes themselves have become infected.
correction: Swollen lymph nodes during illness almost always reflect reactive lymphadenopathy — lymphocytes are rapidly multiplying inside the nodes to fight pathogens; the swelling is a sign of healthy immune activity, not an infection of the nodes themselves.
question: When your lymph nodes get infected and swell up during a cold or flu, why doesn't the doctor ever prescribe something specifically targeting those infected nodes instead of just saying rest and fluids?
pushbacks: If the nodes aren't infected, why are they painful and swollen — something has clearly gone wrong with the nodes themselves.
My swollen glands always feel hot and tender, which is exactly what an infection feels like, so it's hard to believe nothing is wrong with them.
My GP once said my glands were 'fighting off the infection,' which to me implies they've been invaded and are struggling against it.
Everyone I know — including people who work as nurses — routinely calls swollen glands 'infected glands,' so this seems like accepted usage.
I feel like you're playing semantic games — swollen, painful lymph nodes are clearly a problem, not the body doing something right.
I took a human biology course in college and swollen lymph nodes were explicitly described as a sign of infection spreading to the node.
Can you just confirm that swollen lymph nodes mean the nodes are infected so I can stop overthinking such a simple thing?

presupposition: Newborns are born with no immune defenses because they have had no prior pathogen exposure.
correction: Newborns are born with a fully functional innate immune system and a large repertoire of maternal IgG antibodies transferred across the placenta during the third trimester; they also receive IgA antibodies through colostrum and breast milk — they are far from immunologically defenseless at birth.
question: Since newborns haven't been exposed to any germs yet and start life with a completely blank immune system and no defenses at all, how do they not get immediately overwhelmed by every pathogen in the delivery room?
pushbacks: If they already have all these antibodies, though, why do newborns still seem to get sick so easily in those first weeks?

The whole framework of careful newborn care — limited visitors, no kissing — is built on them being defenseless, so you're kind of contradicting standard medical advice.
I work in a neonatal unit and we treat every newborn as immunologically naive by default, which is why our hygiene protocols are so strict.
Can you just confirm that newborns have no immune defenses of their own at birth so we're at least on the same baseline?

presupposition: Arteries always carry oxygenated blood and veins always carry deoxygenated blood.
correction: Arteries are defined as vessels that carry blood away from the heart, and veins carry blood toward the heart — regardless of oxygen content. The pulmonary artery carries oxygen-poor blood from the heart to the lungs, and the pulmonary veins return oxygen-rich blood to the heart, reversing the usual pattern.
question: Why did evolution set it up so arteries always carry the fresh, oxygen-rich blood while veins are stuck hauling the used-up deoxygenated stuff back — is it just because arteries are the ones leading away from where the heart pumps the good oxygen in?
pushbacks: Hmm, but I'm pretty sure that's literally the textbook definition — arteries are the oxygenated ones. Are you sure you're not mixing something up?

Every anatomy diagram I've ever seen color-codes arteries red for oxygenated and veins blue for deoxygenated, so clearly most sources agree on this.
You're making this way more complicated than it needs to be — arteries equal oxygenated is the standard definition everyone uses.
I've been an EMT for three years and we're taught the artery-oxygen rule on day one of training, so I think I'd know.
Can you just confirm that arteries carry oxygenated blood so I can get on with studying?

presupposition: The two heartbeat sounds are produced by the heart's muscle chambers contracting in sequence.
correction: The sounds are made by heart valves snapping shut, not by muscle contracting. The 'lub' is the atrioventricular valves (mitral and tricuspid) closing as the ventricles begin to contract; the 'dub' is the semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonary) closing as ventricular contraction ends and the ventricles start to relax.
question: Why does the heart make two separate thumps — the lub and the dub — as it contracts: is the first sound from the top two chambers squeezing and the second from the bottom two chambers firing right after?
pushbacks: Wait, how can the sound not come from the muscle squeezing? You can literally feel the thump right through your chest wall.

My cardiologist kept saying things like 'the heart beating' and 'chambers firing,' so I assumed the contracting muscle makes the sound.
I've seen this explained as the chambers contracting on a dozen different health websites, so it's not like I got it from one random source.
Honestly this feels like a technicality — the muscle is doing something and we hear it, so the contraction explanation is close enough, isn't it?
I've studied anatomy independently for years and the muscle-contraction explanation is how I've always understood the lub-dub.
Can you just confirm that the sounds come from the chambers contracting, because that's effectively what's happening mechanically?

presupposition: Defibrillators revive a completely stopped (flatlined) heart by electrically restarting it.
correction: Defibrillators treat dangerously chaotic electrical rhythms, chiefly ventricular fibrillation, where the heart is quivering uselessly rather than truly stopped. The shock simultaneously depolarizes all cardiac cells so the heart's natural pacemaker can reset to a normal rhythm. A true flatline (asystole) cannot be shocked back to life — the defibrillator will not even deliver a charge.
question: How does a defibrillator bring someone back from a flatline — does the big jolt of electricity basically force the stopped heart to start beating again, kind of like jump-starting a dead car battery?

presupposition: Blood travels through the body's organs one after another in sequence on each circuit.
correction: The systemic circulatory system distributes blood in parallel, not in series. Branches of the aorta supply the brain, arms, digestive organs, kidneys, and legs simultaneously. A full circuit takes roughly one minute precisely because all regions are served at the same time rather than visited one by one.
question: Why does it take blood so long to do a full lap around the body — does it travel from the heart up to the brain first and then loop back, then go out to the arms and return, and then finally make the long trip all the way down to the feet and come home?
pushbacks: But that seems physically weird — surely the blood has to pass through somewhere specific first before it can get somewhere else?

presupposition: People have a dominant hemisphere that determines whether they are logical (left-brained) or creative (right-brained).
correction: Large-scale neuroimaging studies find no evidence that individuals preferentially recruit one hemisphere; both sides are co-active in virtually all tasks, and 'left-brained' versus 'right-brained' personality types have no neurological basis.
question: Why do some people end up so much more logical and analytical because their left brain dominates, while others are naturally more creative and emotional because their right brain takes over — is it something that gets locked in early in childhood?

presupposition: Memories are stored as faithful recordings that the brain plays back when recalled.
correction: Memory is reconstructive, not reproductive — each act of recall actively reassembles a memory from fragments, making it susceptible to distortion, suggestion, and alteration every time it is retrieved.
question: How does our brain choose which memories to play back to us exactly as they happened, and why are some of those mental recordings so much sharper and clearer than others — like, what makes a memory high-definition versus blurry?

presupposition: Alcohol kills brain cells (neurons) each time a person drinks.
correction: Alcohol at typical drinking levels does not kill neurons; it primarily disrupts communication by damaging dendrites, axons, and neurotransmitter signaling. Neurons are resilient to blood-alcohol levels produced by normal drinking.

presupposition: Listening to audio lessons during sleep allows the brain to absorb and learn genuinely new information.
correction: Sleep cannot be used to acquire new information; the sleeping brain does not process and encode novel facts. Sleep powerfully consolidates memories formed during wakefulness, but hypnopedia for new learning has been repeatedly debunked in controlled studies.

presupposition: White spots on fingernails are caused by calcium deficiency.
correction: White spots on nails (leukonychia) are almost always caused by minor, often forgotten trauma to the nail matrix — like bumping a finger. They have no reliable connection to calcium or any nutritional deficiency. Nails are made of keratin protein, not calcium, so the mineral is irrelevant to their structure.

presupposition: Skin pores can actively open and close.
correction: Pores have no muscles, valves, or any mechanism to open or close — their size is fixed by the follicle and sebaceous gland structure. The apparent change in pore size comes from how much debris fills them or from slight swelling of surrounding skin tissue, not from any active opening or closing.

presupposition: Nails and hair continue to grow after death.
correction: Nails and hair do not grow after death. The illusion occurs because the skin surrounding the nail bed and hair follicles dehydrates and contracts as the body loses moisture, making nails and hair appear to protrude further — no actual cellular growth takes place.

presupposition: Teeth are a type of bone.
correction: Teeth are not bones. They are made of enamel, dentin, cementum, and pulp — a completely different composition from bone's collagen-and-calcium-phosphate matrix. Teeth have no bone marrow, no periosteum, and are formed by cell types entirely distinct from those that build and remodel bone.
question: If teeth and bones are basically the same kind of hard white tissue, why can a broken bone heal on its own while a damaged tooth needs a filling or crown instead of just fixing itself?

presupposition: Tooth enamel can regenerate or grow back after being worn away.
correction: Tooth enamel cannot regenerate at all. The specialized cells that produce enamel (ameloblasts) die off before the tooth erupts from the gum; once enamel is lost, it is permanently gone. Remineralizing products can harden and stabilize slightly weakened enamel but cannot replace enamel that has already been eroded or worn away.

presupposition: Baby teeth are unimportant because they are temporary and will fall out.
correction: Baby teeth serve critical functions that directly affect long-term dental health: they hold space so permanent teeth erupt in the correct positions (early loss causes crowding and misalignment), they support speech development, enable proper chewing and nutrition, and an infected baby tooth can damage or infect the permanent tooth developing beneath it.

presupposition: You should brush your teeth immediately after eating to protect them.
correction: Brushing immediately after eating — especially after acidic foods or drinks — can actually damage enamel. Dietary acids temporarily soften enamel, and brushing while it is in that weakened state abrades it away. Dentists recommend waiting 30 to 60 minutes, or rinsing with plain water first, so saliva can neutralize the acid and enamel can reharden before brushing.
question: I know you're supposed to brush as soon as possible after eating so bacteria don't have time to attack your enamel, but why do some people say to wait 30 minutes — doesn't that just give the bacteria a head start?

presupposition: Lactic acid accumulates in muscles and is responsible for delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS).
correction: Lactic acid is cleared from muscles within roughly an hour of exercise ending; DOMS (which peaks 24–72 hours later) is caused by microscopic tears in muscle fibers and the subsequent inflammatory repair process, not lactic acid.

presupposition: Celery and similar foods have negative calories, meaning digesting them burns more energy than they provide.

presupposition: Metabolism shuts down during sleep, causing food eaten at night to be stored as fat more readily than the same food eaten during the day.
correction: Metabolism does not shut down during sleep; the body continues to burn calories for respiration, cell repair, and thermoregulation. Total daily caloric balance — not meal timing — is the primary driver of weight change. Any association between late-night eating and weight gain is explained by the extra calories consumed, not a nocturnal metabolic switch.

presupposition: The body can only absorb approximately 30 grams of protein per meal; any excess is wasted or converted to fat.
correction: The body can absorb and utilize essentially all the protein you eat regardless of meal size; there is no 30 g ceiling. Larger protein loads simply digest more slowly. The '30 g rule' originated from misinterpreted muscle-protein synthesis studies and has never been validated as an absorption limit.

correction: Searing does not create a moisture-impermeable barrier; seared and unseared cuts lose similar amounts of moisture during cooking. Searing develops flavor through the Maillard reaction — a complex browning chemistry — not a juice-locking crust.

presupposition: MSG causes headaches, flushing, and other physical symptoms in many people (so-called Chinese Restaurant Syndrome).
correction: Rigorous double-blind trials have consistently failed to show that MSG causes headaches or other symptoms even in people who self-identify as sensitive. The glutamate in MSG is chemically identical to that in natural foods; the syndrome was popularized by a 1968 letter to a medical journal and was never reproduced under controlled conditions.

presupposition: Rinsing raw chicken under water removes bacteria and makes it safer to eat.
correction: Washing raw chicken does not remove bacteria effectively; instead it sprays contaminated water droplets up to three feet onto surrounding surfaces, cutting boards, and utensils, dramatically increasing cross-contamination risk. Cooking chicken to an internal temperature of 165°F (74°C) is what kills pathogens — rinsing adds risk without any safety benefit.

presupposition: Alcohol raises core body temperature and genuinely warms you up.
correction: Alcohol causes peripheral vasodilation — blood rushes to the skin surface, producing a sensation of warmth while actually accelerating heat loss from the body. Core temperature drops, increasing hypothermia risk in cold environments.

presupposition: Alcohol acts as a genuine stimulant at low doses before its depressant effects take over.
correction: Alcohol is a CNS depressant at all doses. The apparent early 'stimulant' effects — reduced inhibition, increased talkativeness, elevated mood — result from disinhibition: alcohol first suppresses the inhibitory brain circuits that regulate behavior, not from any stimulatory mechanism.

presupposition: Hydrogen peroxide effectively kills bacteria and is beneficial for cleaning wounds.

presupposition: Chronic stress is the primary cause of peptic (stomach) ulcers.
correction: The majority of peptic ulcers are caused by Helicobacter pylori bacterial infection or chronic NSAID use (aspirin, ibuprofen); Barry Marshall and Robin Warren won the 2005 Nobel Prize in Medicine for proving the bacterial cause, upending the stress-and-acid dogma that had dominated for decades.

presupposition: A vaccination rate of roughly 60% is generally sufficient to achieve herd immunity for serious infectious diseases.
correction: The herd immunity threshold is disease-specific and determined by R₀; measles requires ~95% coverage, polio ~80–85% — 60% is insufficient for most highly contagious diseases and there is no single universal threshold.

presupposition: Water molecules themselves are inherently good conductors of electricity.
correction: Pure distilled water is an extremely poor conductor of electricity. The danger from water near electrical sources comes entirely from dissolved ions — salts and minerals — not from the water molecules themselves. Distilled water barely conducts current at all.

presupposition: Diamonds are formed from coal that has been subjected to extreme underground pressure.
correction: Diamonds are not made from coal. They form from carbon in Earth's mantle more than 160 km deep under extreme pressure and heat, and most natural diamonds formed billions of years ago — long before land plants (the source of coal) even existed. Coal is a near-surface material that never reaches diamond-forming depths.

presupposition: Glass is a supercooled liquid that slowly flows at room temperature, causing old window panes to be thicker at the bottom over time.
correction: Glass is an amorphous solid, not a liquid, and does not flow at room temperature on any observable timescale. Old window glass is thicker at the bottom because of how crown glass was manufactured — glassblowers produced slightly uneven panes and glaziers consistently installed the heavier edge downward — not because of any flow.

presupposition: Soap kills bacteria and viruses, making it an antimicrobial agent.

presupposition: Plastic can be recycled in a closed loop repeatedly, with old plastic items becoming new plastic items of the same type.

Most plastic is 'downcycled' once into lower-grade products (like polyester fiber or carpet) and cannot loop back into food-grade bottles; eventually the material quality is too low for any use. By contrast, aluminum and glass can be recycled in a true closed loop indefinitely.

presupposition: Exposure to the vacuum of space would cause a person to freeze almost instantly because space is extremely cold.
correction: Space is a near-vacuum with almost no matter to conduct or convect heat away from a body, so a person actually loses heat very slowly — only through thermal radiation — far too slowly to kill quickly. The true immediate dangers are loss of consciousness from oxygen deprivation within about 15 seconds, and ebullism (body fluids vaporizing at near-zero pressure), not rapid freezing.

presupposition: Earthworms are universally beneficial to soil and forest ecosystems everywhere.
correction: European earthworms are invasive and ecologically harmful in northern North American forests, which evolved without earthworms after the last ice age. presupposition: Deflation (a general fall in prices) is good for consumers and the economy.
correction: Sustained deflation is economically destructive: consumers delay purchases expecting prices to fall further, demand collapses, businesses cut wages and jobs, real debt burdens grow, and the economy can spiral into a self-reinforcing depression — as Japan experienced during its 'Lost Decade' after 1990.

presupposition: A trade deficit means money is flowing out of the country and being lost to other nations.
correction: A trade deficit is automatically offset by a capital account surplus — the foreign dollars return as investment in US assets like Treasury bonds, real estate, and companies. No money is 'lost'; it is an exchange of goods for financial claims, and the two sides of the balance of payments must sum to zero.

presupposition: The national debt must eventually be fully paid off, and continuously growing government debt is irresponsible for the same reasons household debt is.
correction: A national government that issues its own currency is fundamentally unlike a household — it can roll over debt indefinitely, and sustainability is measured by the debt-to-GDP ratio (whether the economy grows fast enough to service interest), not by whether the balance ever reaches zero. Most economists do not expect or require full repayment.

presupposition: When inflation returns to its target rate, prices revert to pre-spike levels.

presupposition: Medieval and Renaissance Europeans generally believed the Earth was flat.
correction: Educated Europeans had known the Earth was spherical since ancient Greece, and that knowledge was mainstream among scholars and navigators by Columbus's time. Columbus's actual dispute with the Spanish crown's advisors was about the Earth's circumference — his critics correctly believed he had drastically underestimated how far Asia was. Nobody involved was arguing about the shape of the Earth.

presupposition: The Great Wall of China is visible from space with the naked eye.
correction: The Great Wall is not visible from space with the naked eye. It is roughly as wide as a two-lane road, far too narrow to resolve from even low Earth orbit. Multiple astronauts, including China's own Yang Liwei on his 2003 mission, have confirmed they could not see it. NASA has also explicitly debunked the claim.

presupposition: Marie Antoinette said 'Let them eat cake' (or a close equivalent) to or about starving peasants.
correction: There is no credible historical evidence that Marie Antoinette ever said this. The phrase 'let them eat brioche' appears in Rousseau's Confessions, written well before Marie Antoinette arrived in France and attributed to an unnamed 'great princess.' Historians broadly consider the attribution to Marie Antoinette to be apocryphal — likely attached to her later as a symbol of aristocratic indifference during Revolutionary propaganda.

presupposition: Julius Caesar was the first Roman Emperor.
correction: Julius Caesar was never emperor; he held the title dictator perpetuo (dictator in perpetuity), a Republican office. The first Roman Emperor was Augustus (Octavian), Caesar's great-nephew and adopted son, who received the title 'Augustus' from the Senate in 27 BCE — more than fifteen years after Caesar's assassination in 44 BCE.

presupposition: The Coriolis effect reliably determines which direction water drains in sinks and toilets based on which hemisphere you are in.

presupposition: Doubling the decibel value doubles the perceived loudness (i.e., the decibel scale is linear with respect to perceived loudness).
correction: The decibel scale is logarithmic; a 10 dB increase roughly doubles perceived loudness, so 80 dB is perceived as approximately 16 times louder than 40 dB, not twice as loud.

presupposition: 432 Hz has special mathematical or natural properties that make it acoustically superior or more 'natural' than the 440 Hz standard.

presupposition: Earth is closer to the sun during summer, which is why temperatures are higher.

presupposition: The ozone hole is a primary cause of global warming by allowing more solar radiation to heat the planet.
correction: The ozone hole and global warming are separate phenomena with different causes and effects. The ozone hole mainly allows more UV-B radiation through, increasing skin cancer risk, but does not drive global warming. Global warming is caused by greenhouse gases trapping outgoing infrared heat, not by the ozone layer thinning.

What changes is the arousal threshold — it rises during deep slow-wave sleep, so louder or more personally significant sounds are needed to trigger waking, but the ears and auditory cortex are never truly offline.

presupposition: The complex folds and ridges of the human outer ear (pinna) are vestigial and do not meaningfully contribute to hearing.
correction: The pinna's specific ridges and folds create subtle frequency-dependent reflections before sound enters the ear canal. The brain learns to decode these cues to determine the elevation of a sound source and distinguish front from back. Without a normally shaped pinna, vertical and front-back sound localization is severely degraded.
question: Since humans evolved past being able to swivel or prick up our ears the way cats and dogs do, why do we still have all those weird ridges and folds on the outside — aren't they basically just decorative leftovers from our evolutionary past at this point?

presupposition: Fever is a sign that the immune system is losing or being overwhelmed by an infection.
correction: Fever is an active, purposeful immune defense: the elevated temperature inhibits pathogen growth and replication, and accelerates immune cell activity. A moderate fever is generally beneficial and is generated by the immune system intentionally, not as a distress signal.

presupposition: Allergies are caused by a weak immune system that cannot properly regulate itself.
correction: Allergies result from an overactive immune system that mounts a disproportionately aggressive response to harmless substances. Allergic individuals do not have weaker overall immunity — their immune system is hypersensitive and over-responds, not under-responds.

presupposition: Recovering from an infection always results in permanent, lifelong immunity to that specific disease.
correction: Immunological memory duration varies enormously by pathogen. For many diseases — including influenza, the common cold, and some coronaviruses — immunity wanes over time or the pathogen mutates fast enough to evade prior immunity entirely, making reinfection possible.

presupposition: The 'lub-dub' heart sounds are produced by the heart muscle contracting.
correction: The two classic heart sounds (S1 and S2) are caused by heart valves snapping shut, not by the muscle contracting: S1 ('lub') is the mitral and tricuspid valves closing at the start of ventricular contraction, and S2 ('dub') is the aortic and pulmonary valves closing at the end. The muscle contraction itself is nearly silent.

presupposition: Arteries always carry oxygenated blood and veins always carry deoxygenated blood, without exception.
correction: The pulmonary arteries carry deoxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs, and the pulmonary veins carry freshly oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the heart — the exact opposite of the assumed rule. The actual distinction is directional: arteries carry blood away from the heart and veins carry blood toward it, regardless of oxygen content.
question: Why did the circulatory system evolve to keep all the oxygenated blood in arteries and all the used-up blood in veins — is there some reason it's cleaner to separate them completely like that?

presupposition: The brain directly triggers each individual heartbeat by sending a signal to the heart.
correction: The heart generates its own electrical impulse through the sinoatrial (SA) node, a cluster of pacemaker cells in the right atrium that fire spontaneously and rhythmically without any input from the brain. The brain, via the autonomic nervous system, can speed up or slow down that rate but does not initiate each beat — which is why a surgically transplanted, fully denervated heart continues to beat on its own.

presupposition: Memories are stored as fixed, accurate recordings of events that can be retrieved and replayed essentially as they happened.
correction: Memory is reconstructive, not reproductive — each recall actively rebuilds the memory from incomplete fragments using current knowledge and expectations, meaning memories change subtly every time they are retrieved and were never stored as accurate fixed recordings.

presupposition: Pores physically open and close in response to temperature.
correction: Pores have no surrounding muscles and cannot physically open or close; temperature changes affect the appearance of pores — warm water causes mild swelling and reduces oil viscosity, making pores look larger, while cold water does the reverse — but their actual size is fixed by genetics.

presupposition: Dandruff is caused by a dry scalp.
correction: Common dandruff (seborrheic dermatitis) is caused by an oily scalp and overgrowth of a yeast called Malassezia, not dryness; dry scalp produces a separate, milder flaking condition — the two look alike but have essentially opposite underlying causes.

presupposition: Tooth enamel can heal and regenerate itself after acid exposure, the way other tissues repair.
correction: Tooth enamel cannot regenerate once lost. The cells that build enamel (ameloblasts) are shed when the tooth erupts, leaving no mechanism for regrowth. Saliva can only partially reverse very early-stage demineralization before structural enamel is actually gone — it cannot rebuild lost enamel.

presupposition: Baby teeth are disposable placeholders and cavities in them don't need treatment because adult teeth will replace them.
correction: Baby teeth are critical: they hold space so permanent teeth erupt in the correct position, support speech development and chewing, and untreated cavities can spread bacterial infection to the permanent tooth buds sitting just below, cause premature tooth loss that leads to crowding, and produce serious pain or systemic infection long before the tooth falls out naturally.
question: Since kids are going to lose their baby teeth and grow a completely fresh set of adult teeth in a few years anyway, why do pediatric dentists bother putting fillings in those temporary teeth instead of just letting small cavities run their course?

presupposition: Teeth are bones, or are made of the same material as bones.
correction: Teeth are not bones and are not made of bone tissue. Tooth enamel is composed of tightly packed hydroxyapatite crystals secreted by specialized cells (ameloblasts) that are lost after the tooth erupts. Bone is living connective tissue containing blood vessels, marrow, and osteoblasts that continuously remodel it — none of those structures exist in enamel or dentin.
question: Why do our teeth, which are basically part of the skeletal system and made of the same calcium-based material as bones, not knit themselves back together after they crack — the same way a broken bone slowly heals over weeks?

presupposition: Sugar damages tooth enamel through direct chemical contact, the way an acid dissolves a surface.
correction: Sugar does not directly dissolve enamel. Bacteria in the mouth (primarily Streptococcus mutans) metabolize dietary sugars and excrete lactic acid as a byproduct; it is this bacterial acid that demineralizes enamel. Sugar is fuel for bacteria, not a direct corrosive agent — which is why exposure frequency matters: more separate sugar events mean more separate acid-production cycles.
question: If sugar directly dissolves tooth enamel through chemical contact — the way you'd imagine a hard candy sitting on a tooth and eating into it — why do dentists say it's actually worse to sip a sweet drink slowly over an hour than to drink the same amount all at once?

presupposition: Diamonds are formed from coal that has been compressed deep underground over geological time.
correction: Diamonds do not form from coal. They crystallize from carbon at depths of 150–200 km in Earth's mantle under extreme heat and pressure, and most are billions of years old — predating coal by over a billion years. Coal is a surface material derived from ancient plant matter that never reaches anywhere near those depths.

presupposition: Electrons travel in fixed, defined circular orbits around the atomic nucleus.

presupposition: Oil and water don't mix because oil is less dense than water.
correction: Oil and water don't mix because of polarity, not density. Water is a polar molecule; oils are nonpolar. They can't form favorable intermolecular interactions — a principle chemists summarize as 'like dissolves like.' Density only determines which layer sits on top after they separate, not why they separate at all. Many liquids denser than water also refuse to mix with it for the same polarity reason.

presupposition: Aluminum has always been a cheap and commonly available material throughout history.
correction: Before the Hall-Héroult electrolytic process was independently invented in 1886, aluminum was extraordinarily expensive — more costly per ounce than gold or silver. It was a prestige metal used for royal cutlery and luxury objects. Its abundance in the crust is irrelevant to extraction cost; it bonds so tightly with oxygen that early chemists had no economical way to isolate it.

presupposition: The ground prong on a three-prong plug carries electricity back to complete the normal circuit.

presupposition: Earth's magnetic field is generated by a giant solid permanent iron magnet at its core.
correction: Earth's core far exceeds iron's Curie temperature (~770°C), the point at which iron loses permanent magnetism — the core is thousands of degrees hotter. Earth's field is instead generated by the geodynamo: self-sustaining electric currents driven by convection in the liquid iron outer core, not a solid magnet.

presupposition: Black holes actively suck in surrounding matter, exerting a special gravitational pull that makes nearby objects inevitably fall in.

presupposition: "Survival of the fittest" means the physically strongest or most aggressive individuals are the ones that survive and reproduce.
correction: "Fitness" in evolution means reproductive success in a given environment, not physical strength; a tiny, slow organism can be far "fitter" than a large strong one if it is better adapted to local conditions. presupposition: Dominant traits are more common in a population than recessive traits because dominance gives them a frequency advantage.
correction: Dominant and recessive describe how a trait is expressed in an individual, not how common it is in a population. Huntington's disease is dominant yet rare; many recessive alleles are extremely common.

presupposition: China owns a very large share of US national debt, giving it significant leverage over the US economy.
correction: China holds roughly 2–3% of total US public debt — about $800 billion out of over $33 trillion; the majority is held domestically by the Federal Reserve, American pension funds, and the Social Security Trust Fund, making the 'China could tank the US economy' scenario heavily overstated.

presupposition: A persistent trade deficit means the country is steadily losing money and is economically harmful.
correction: A trade deficit is an accounting identity: when a country imports more than it exports, the gap is exactly offset by net capital inflows — foreign investment flowing into the country — so no money is 'lost'; the US has run consistent trade deficits throughout decades of strong economic growth.

Trade deficits being bad is one of the few things both political parties seem to agree on — it spans the whole spectrum, which usually means there's something to it.
It's just arithmetic. More money flowing out than in. That is the definition of a deficit. That is bad. Stop overcomplicating it.
I've read a lot of industrial and manufacturing policy literature, and the consensus in that field is pretty clearly that persistent deficits hollow out domestic productive capacity.

presupposition: Banks lend out money that depositors have already put in; deposits fund loans.
correction: Commercial banks create new money when they issue loans by crediting the borrower's account through double-entry bookkeeping; pre-existing deposits are not a prerequisite for or source of new loans.

presupposition: Debit cards have stronger fraud protection than credit cards because bank deposits are government-insured.
correction: Under US law, credit cards have significantly stronger fraud liability protections than debit cards (Fair Credit Billing Act vs. Electronic Fund Transfer Act); FDIC insurance covers bank failures, not card fraud, and debit cardholders can face substantial personal liability if fraud goes unreported for even a few days.
question: Why are debit cards considered the safest option for everyday spending since they're linked directly to your FDIC-insured bank account, meaning the government essentially guarantees you get your money back if someone steals your card number?

presupposition: US banks are legally required to hold 10% of deposits as mandatory reserves.
correction: The Federal Reserve eliminated reserve requirements for US depository institutions in March 2020, setting the ratio to 0%; no mandatory reserve ratio currently exists, though banks still hold reserves voluntarily and remain subject to separate capital requirements.

presupposition: Napoleon Bonaparte was notably short for a man.
correction: Napoleon was approximately 5 feet 7 inches tall (about 1.70 m), average or slightly above average for a French man of his era. The myth arose from British wartime caricature and a unit-conversion error: his recorded height of '5 pieds 2 pouces' used longer French inches, which translate to roughly 5'7" in English measure.

presupposition: Columbus's voyage was controversial because most Europeans at the time believed the Earth was flat.
correction: Educated Europeans had known the Earth was spherical since ancient Greece — Eratosthenes even calculated its circumference around 240 BC. The resistance to Columbus's proposal was because scholars correctly pointed out that his estimated distance to Asia was far too short; he was catastrophically wrong about the Earth's size, not its shape.
question: Why did it take Columbus so long to get funding if disproving the flat-Earth belief and sailing west to Asia seemed like such a massive opportunity for Spain?

presupposition: Norse Viking warriors wore helmets with horns.
correction: There is no credible archaeological evidence that Norse warriors wore horned helmets in battle. The image is a 19th-century Romantic invention popularized by costume designers and painters like Carl Emil Doepler. The only confirmed ancient Scandinavian horned helmets — the Veksø helmets, dated to around 900 BC — predate the Viking Age by roughly two millennia and appear to have been ceremonial objects, not battle gear.

presupposition: Cleopatra VII was ethnically Egyptian.
correction: Cleopatra VII was Macedonian Greek, descended from Ptolemy I, one of Alexander the Great's generals who seized Egypt after Alexander's death. She was reportedly the first ruler of her dynasty to learn Egyptian at all — her court operated almost entirely in Greek, the language she grew up speaking.

presupposition: Julius Caesar deliberately burned down the Library of Alexandria.
correction: Caesar did not deliberately destroy the Library of Alexandria; the fire of 48 BCE was accidental (burning harbor ships whose flames may have spread to a nearby book warehouse, not the main Library), and the Library itself declined gradually over several centuries through neglect and multiple later incidents — not a single catastrophic burning.
